title: Exploring & Cleaning the Data
concept: Exploratory Data Analysis (EDA) means examining a raw dataset for missing values, inconsistent formats, and outliers before any modeling begins.

exercises.prompt: Recap: What is the target variable in StreamBox's churn project?
exercises.explain: Exactly! churned: yes/no is the binary label the model learns to predict.

cards.title: Opening the spreadsheet
cards.body: StreamBox's data team pulls the raw churn dataset and... it's a mess. Some rows are missing a last login date entirely. Signup dates are written three different ways. And one customer supposedly watched 9,999 hours last month — more than 13 hours a day, every day, times two.

cards.title: Exploratory Data Analysis (EDA)
cards.body: EDA means examining a raw dataset — before training anything — to spot problems like missing values, inconsistent formats, and outliers that could quietly corrupt the model.

cards.title: Checking the fridge before cooking
cards.body: A chef checks every ingredient before cooking: is anything spoiled (missing), mislabeled (inconsistent format), or clearly wrong, like a carton marked "5,000 eggs" (an outlier)? EDA is that same inspection, done on data instead of food.

cards.title: Three problems in StreamBox's data
cards.body: The team finds: last_login is blank for 12,400 of 500,000 rows (2.5%). signup_date appears as 01/15/2023, 2023-01-15, and Jan 15, 2023 — three formats mixed in one column. And watch_hours has entries above 720 — the maximum possible hours in a 30-day month — which are physically impossible outliers.

cards.title: "The model will figure it out" — it won't
cards.body: You might assume a model can just ignore messy data on its own. In reality, mixed date formats and impossible outliers actively mislead training — remember "garbage in, garbage out" from Feature Engineering? Cleaning has to happen first.

cards.title: Messy vs. Cleaned
cards.body: The same data, before and after EDA:

exercises.prompt: What does EDA stand for in a data science workflow?
exercises.explain: Correct! EDA means examining a dataset for problems before modeling begins.

exercises.prompt: True or false: A watch_hours value of 9,999 in a 30-day month is a reasonable value the model should learn from as-is.
exercises.explain: False! A 30-day month has at most 720 hours total, so 9,999 is an impossible outlier that needs to be capped or investigated, not trusted as-is.

exercises.sentence: Before training any model, StreamBox must ___ the data to fix missing values, inconsistent formats, and outliers.
exercises.explain: Cleaning the data — fixing missing values, formats, and outliers — always comes before modeling.

exercises.prompt: Which of these values from StreamBox's raw data is the outlier?
exercises.explain: Correct! 9,999 hours is physically impossible in a 30-day month (max 720), making it a clear outlier.

exercises.prompt: StreamBox's dataset has 12,400 rows with a blank last_login, a signup_date column mixing three date formats, and some watch_hours values above 720 (impossible in a 30-day month). What should the data team do before training a model?

options.text: Flag or fill the missing last_login values, standardize signup_date into one consistent format, and cap or investigate the impossible watch_hours outliers.
Delete the entire dataset and start over, since messy data can never be used.
Leave everything as-is — the model will automatically ignore bad values.
Only fix the missing values; date formats and outliers don't affect model training.

exercises.explain: Exactly! Missing values, inconsistent formats, and outliers all need to be addressed with the cleaning techniques from Tabular Processing. Next: turning this cleaned data into features the model can actually learn from!
